one_line_takeaway: ProGRank reranks RAG retrieval candidates using gradient-instability signals from randomized perturbations, gated to the high-score region, cutting judge-based downstream attack success rate to 0.000 across three datasets against optimization-driven poisoning attacks while carrying only a dataset-dependent clean-utility cost and 4.73s/query overhead.
key_finding: On retrieval-stage metrics, ProGRank reduces both Poison Hit Rate and Poison Recall Rate relative to the undefended base score, with the strongest effect on HotpotQA and Top-50 Poison Hit Rate reduced to around 30% on NQ and MS MARCO. In downstream generation (Table 2, Top-5), ProGRank achieves the lowest macro-average judge-based ASR on all three datasets, reaching 0.000 on HotpotQA, NQ, and MS MARCO, and the lowest macro-average substring-based ASR of 0.011 (HotpotQA), 0.000 (NQ), and 0.033 (MS MARCO), versus an undefended baseline macro-average judge-based ASR of 0.912 (HotpotQA), 0.756 (NQ), 0.891 (MS MARCO). For clean utility (Table 5), ProGRank gives the best NQ EM (0.225 vs. baseline 0.175), while the undefended baseline/RAGuard perform best on HotpotQA F1 (0.431/0.440 vs. ProGRank's 0.304) and GRADA gives the best MS MARCO ROUGE-L F1 (0.251 vs. ProGRank's 0.202) -- i.e., a dataset-dependent robustness-utility trade-off, not uniform superiority. Latency (Table 4): ProGRank averages 4.73 s/query, versus GRADA 0.90 s/query (faster), GMTP 14.60 s/query, and RAGuard 118.17 s/query (about 20x slower than ProGRank, attributed to RAGuard's repeated leave-one-out verification). Under ProGRank-targeted adaptive/evasive attacks (Table 3), macro-average substring-ASR/judge-ASR/ACC were 0.030/0.016/0.372 (HotpotQA), 0.016/0.034/0.365 (NQ), 0.049/0.019/0.429 (MS MARCO), which the authors present as evidence the defence remains effective under the adaptive settings they tested. Ablation results show the full gated objective performs best overall, and removing the dispersion-risk penalty causes a larger degradation than removing the representational-consistency penalty; performance is reported stable across R sweeps.
core_contribution: ProGRank is a post hoc, training-free retriever-side reranking defence for dense-retriever RAG against corpus poisoning. For each query-passage pair it applies stochastic perturbations (token dropout, encoder dropout, or a mixture) over R repeated runs, computes 'probe gradients' of the retriever similarity score with respect to a small fixed parameter subset (a LayerNorm module at a chosen layer L), and derives two instability statistics from these gradients: representational consistency (Rep, a normalized gradient signal-to-noise ratio measuring directional agreement across perturbation runs) and dispersion risk (DR, a lower-quantile-aggregated deviation statistic emphasizing lower-tail instability). These are converted into additive penalties and combined via a sigmoid score-gate wθ(q,p) centered on the upper-tail (near-Top-K) region of the base-score distribution, so robustness correction is concentrated on decision-critical high-score candidates rather than applied uniformly. The final defended score is sθ(q,p) minus the gated, penalty-weighted correction. The method requires no retraining, preserves original passage content (does not filter or rewrite passages), and supports a surrogate-retriever variant when the deployed retriever's internals are unavailable.
threat_model: Corpus poisoning against dense-retriever RAG: an adversary injects or edits a small number of passages in a web-scale or user-contributed retrieval corpus so that they are ranked into the Top-K results for target queries, thereby steering downstream LLM generation toward attacker-chosen (incorrect or harmful) answers. The paper targets optimization-driven poisons specifically -- passages whose high retrieval score is achieved by concentrating on a small set of perturbation-sensitive matching features (e.g., via HotFlip-style token optimization or joint retrieval+generation objectives) rather than distributed, robust semantic similarity. The defence sits at the retriever/reranking stage, does not modify the generator, and does not retrain the retriever; it explicitly does not assume all poisoned passages are intrinsically unstable, only that instability under perturbation is a useful reranking signal.
limitations: The paper's own conclusion and text note: (1) the core hypothesis is not that all poisoned passages are intrinsically unstable under perturbation -- only that instability is a useful signal for a 'practically important subset' of optimization-driven poisons; passages that do not rely on concentrated, perturbation-sensitive features may evade detection. (2) The retrieval-stage objective is explicitly stated to be a proxy, not a complete end-to-end safety objective. (3) Evaluation is confined to controlled candidate-pool reranking (a fixed 50-clean-passage pool per query plus injected poisons); the authors state future work is needed for larger-scale deployment with approximate-nearest-neighbor first-stage retrieval and different top-B pool sizes, meaning scalability to web-scale corpora is not demonstrated. (4) Clean-utility results are mixed/dataset-dependent -- ProGRank is not the best on HotpotQA F1 or MS MARCO ROUGE-L F1, showing a real robustness-utility trade-off rather than a free win. (5) The mechanism-level justification (Sec 3.4, the Bernoulli-gate decomposition of gradients into stable + concentrated components) is presented as an informal abstraction/intuition explaining an empirical trend, not a formal guarantee. (6) The adaptive-attack evaluation (Table 3) is limited to 'the adaptive attack configurations considered in this work' (ProGRank-based evasive attacks the authors themselves construct), so robustness against unknown or future adaptive attack strategies is untested. (7) Baseline numbers for GRADA/GMTP/RAGuard were re-measured under the authors' own unified pipeline and may differ from the original papers' reported numbers -- a comparability caveat the authors themselves flag.

quantitative_results: Downstream macro-avg judge-based ASR at Top-5: ProGRank 0.000 on HotpotQA, NQ, and MS MARCO vs. undefended baseline 0.912 (HotpotQA), 0.756 (NQ), 0.891 (MS MARCO)
Downstream macro-avg substring-based ASR: ProGRank 0.011 (HotpotQA), 0.000 (NQ), 0.033 (MS MARCO) vs. baseline 0.882 (HotpotQA), 0.678 (NQ), 0.799 (MS MARCO)

Clean utility (Table 5): NQ EM -- ProGRank 0.225 (best) vs. baseline/RAGuard 0.175; HotpotQA F1 -- baseline 0.440/RAGuard 0.431 (best) vs. ProGRank 0.304; MS MARCO ROUGE-L F1 -- GRADA 0.251 (best) vs. ProGRank 0.202
Latency (Table 4, mean s/query): GRADA 0.90, GMTP 14.60, RAGuard 118.17, ProGRank 4.73 (RAGuard ~20x slower than ProGRank)
Retrieval-stage: Top-50 Poison Hit Rate reduced to around 30% on NQ and MS MARCO under ProGRank vs. undefended base score; strongest reduction observed on HotpotQA
Fixed hyperparameters used for main results: R=20 perturbation repeats, probe layer L=3, mixed perturbation, tau=0.1, alpha=4.0, C=6.0, K=5
baselines_compared: GMTP (gradient-based masked token probability detector, Findings of ACL 2025)
